Facts.text: Bulgarian Saint John of Rila [Saint Ivan] performs the miracle of the two pies - uses two pies to feed the poor

War elephant corps of the Southern Han defeated at Shao by crossbow fire from Song Dynasty troops forcing the Southern Han state to submit to the Song Dynasty. First regular war elephant corps in the Chinese army.
First English Parliament not summoned by the monarch formally convenes
First printing of Ramban's Sha'ar ha-Gemul
Having published nothing for eleven years, Francois Rabelais publishes the Tiers Livre, his sequel to Gargantua and Pantagruel
Shaanxi Earthquake, the deadliest ever recorded, kills 830,000 in Shaanxi Province, China
The Regent of Scotland, the Earl of Moray, is assassinated, triggering a civil war
Union of Utrecht signed by Northern Habsburg counties of Holland and Zeeland, and provinces of Utrecht and Groningen, marking the beginning of protestant Dutch Republic
France and Sweden sign the anti-German Treaty of Bärwalde
Dutch governor Johan Mauritius lands in Pernambuco, Brazil
England, Netherlands & Sweden sign Triple Alliance against French
Liechtenstein becomes a member state of the Holy Roman Empire
Niccolò Piccinni's tragédie lyrique opera "Iphigénie en Tauride" (Iphigeneia in Tauris) first performed by Académie royale de musique at the Paris Opéra
Georgetown, the first US Catholic college, is founded
Humane Society of Philadelphia is organized (first aid society)
Prussia and Russia sign the Treaty of Grodno, later known as the 2nd Partition of Poland, ceding large portions of Poland to their bigger neighbors
Armand-Gaston Camus becomes chairman of the Council of 500
7.8 earthquake shakes New Madrid, Missouri
Joseph Pease becomes the first Quaker to be admitted to the UK Parliament on his affirmation
Uniform US election day for President & VP authorized
Tunisia becomes the first Arab nation to outlaw slavery in decree issued by Ahmed I Bey
US Patent granted for an envelope-making machine to Jesse K. Park and Cornelius S. Watson
First bridge over the Mississippi River in what is now Minneapolis, Minnesota, opens; today known as the Father Louis Hennepin Bridge
American sidewheel steamer SS Pacific leaves Liverpool, England on what becomes her final voyage; lost at sea enroute to New York with 186 on board
Mauna Loa volcano in Hawaii begins an eruption that lasts 300 days
Agoston Haraszthy, the first vintner in Sonoma Valley, California, imports 10,000 grapevine cuttings
-Jan 25th) Battle of City Point, Virginia (James River, Trent's Reach)

Marias Massacre: Approximately 200 Piegan Blackfeet Indians (mostly women, children, and elderly men) killed by US Army, in Montana Territory, spawning outrage and preventing the military from regaining control of the Bureau of Indian Affairs
US National Archery Association forms in Crawfordsville, Indiana
G. W. Bunbury of Dublin sets shorthand record of 250 wpm for 10 min
Edward Macdowell's 2nd Suite in E premieres

Elva Zona Heaster is found dead in Greenbrier County, West Virginia. Resulting murder trial of her husband perhaps only case in US history where the alleged testimony of a ghost helped secure a conviction.
First radio rescue at sea using the CQD distress code by the British Royal Mail steamship Republic off Nantucket Island
The International Opium Convention is signed at The Hague
Temperature falls from 44°F (7°C) to -56°F (49°C) night of 23-24, Browning, Montana
Dutch refuse to turn over ex-Emperor William II of Germany to allies
Taxi strike in Amsterdam begins (through March 9th)
Clyde Tombaugh photographs dwarf planet Pluto
George Washington Birthplace National Monument, Virginia established
WIS-AM (now WOMG) in Columbia SC begins radio transmissions
El Salvador army kills 4,000 protesting farmers
20th amendment, which changed the date of US presidential inaugurations to 20th January, is ratified
New York Rangers' left wing Murray Murdoch plays his 400th straight NHL game in a 5-2 win over Ottawa Senators at Madison Square Garden, NYC; streak reaches 508 consecutive games
Catholic People's Party (KVP) of Curacao forms
1st mass killing of Jews in Romania during three days of riots, between 115 and 130 people are murdered
Groundbreaking for NACA (now NASA) Lewis Research Center
WOR-AM in Newark NJ moves to NYC
Battle of Rabaul: Japanese attack Australian air base in Rabaul, New Britain
Tank battle at Adzjedabia, Africa Korp vs British 8th army
Detroit Red Wings scores NHL record 8 goals in 1 period
Japanese Mount Austen on Guadalcanal captured
The fall of Tripoli: British 8th army and Commonwealth forces march into Tripoli, as the German army is pushed back to Tunisia
Detroit Red Wings score 15 goals against New York Rangers with an NHL record 37 points; also record consecutive goals & most lopsided game, 15-0
Dutch Prime Minister Pieter Sjoerds Gerbrandy, exiled in London, tenders his resignation
Rear Admiral Sidney W. Souers, USNR, becomes 1st director of CIA
3rd edition of Joseph Kane's Famous 1st Facts published
AP picks "Miracle Braves" of 1914 as greatest sports upset
Israeli Knesset declares Jerusalem the capital of Israel
NFL rule changes open way for 2-platoon system (offense & defense)
Rebel army of Dutch military officer Raymond Westerling occupies Bandung, Java
3rd Emmy Awards: Alan Young Show, Alan Young and Gertrude Berg win
NFL Dallas Texans become Baltimore Colts (now Indianapolis Colts)
NFL's National & American conference become Eastern & Western conference
Equal longest undefeated streak in Toronto Maple Leaf history ends - 18 games, 16 wins, 2 ties
Harry van Thorn chosen chairman of Dutch KVP
Express train travelling from York to Bristol derails, killing 14 and injuring many other passengers
KORK (now KVBC) TV channel 3 in Las Vegas, Nevada (NBC) 1st broadcast
Indian nationalist V. K. Krishna Menon begins the longest ever speech defending India's stand on Kashmir at the UN - 8 hours over 2 days

Wham-O Company acquires rights to the "Pluto Platter" plastic flying disc toy from inventor Walter Frederick Morrison; they rename it "Frisbee" in 1958 as a nod to the Frisbie Pie Company, whose empty tins were tossed by Yale University students (including Morrison) in the 1930s
Dictator Marcos Perez Jiménez flees Venezuela, Larrazabal takes power

US Navy bathyscaphe "Trieste", crewed by Swiss oceanographer Jacques Piccard and USN Lieutenant Don Walsh, becomes first manned vessel to reach bottom of Pacific Ocean (10,916 m) in the Mariana Trench; craft was designed by Auguste Piccard, father of the pilot, in 1953 for the French Navy
US Supreme Court rules cities and states have the right to censor films
Venezuela adopts constitution
Film "Jules et Jim", based on the novel by Henri-Pierre Roché, directed by François Truffaut and starring Jeanne Moreau is released
Libya, Morocco, Algeria. and Tunisia plan to form United Arab Maghreb; planning continues until an agreement is reached in 1989
24th Amendment to the United States Constitution ratified, barring poll tax in federal elections
Musical variety program "King Family Show", featuring big band vocal group The King Sisters, premieres on ABC TV
Spy ship USS Pueblo & 83-man crew seized in Sea of Japan by North Korea
Cream releases their last album "Goodbye"
Australia's 1st amateur radio satellite (Oscar 5) launched (California)
Member of Dutch feminist group Dolle Mina burn their bras in Amsterdam
US launches second generation weather satellite, ITOS 1
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
-80°F (-62°C) in Prospect Creek Camp, Alaska (US record)
Britain allowed to sell arms to South Africa, after Commonwealth Conference in Singapore ends in compromise
Riots break out in the Shankill Road area of Belfast, North Ireland
UCLA loses to Notre Dame, UCLA then wins next 88 games in a row
Bootlegger sells wood alcohol to a wedding party, killing 100 in New Delhi
Entire population of Istanbul is placed under 24 hour house arrest
Boeing 707, operated by Alia Royal Jordanian and chartered by Nigeria Airways crashes while trying to land at Kano International Airport in Nigeria, killing 176 Muslim pilgrims and crew
Helgafell volcano on island of Heimaey Iceland erupts for 1st time in 7,000 yrs
1st edition of women's magazine "Story"
"Barney Miller" premieres on ABC TV
Ian Redpath hits his only 2 sixes in Cricket Tests, v WI Adelaide
The Provisional Irish Republican Army truce of February 1975 is officially brought to an end

The results to U.S. advice columnist Ann Landers' question (if they could do it all over, would parents still become parents) state that 70% of 10,000 responding parents were negative about being parents
Washington Capitals end 25 game winless streak (0-22-3) beat NY Rangers 7-5
$1.5-million Serge Lepage dress exhibited, Paris
Miniseries "Roots" premieres on ABC
Belgian industrial Haron Empain kidnapped in Paris
Sweden becomes the first nation in the world to ban aerosol sprays, due to their harmful effect on the earth's ozone layer.
1st Richard Nixon museum opens in San Clemente California
Jochem Bird elected mayor of West Berlin
Urbe Blanca (cow) produces record 110 kg of milk, Cuba (approx date)
World Airways DC-10 skids at Boston Logan Airport killing 2
Cerebral Palsy telethon raises $14.7 million
Russian radioactive satellite falls into Indian Ocean
Schone skates ladies world record 5 km (7:40.97)
Buffalo Sabres become the first team in NHL history to win 10 straight road games with a 5-3 victory at Boston
Greatest unpaced 1-hr bicycle distance, F Moser (Italy), 51.15 km
Britains House of Lords debate 1st televised
Columbia returns to Kennedy Space Center via Davis-Monthan AFB
Dow Jones Industrial Average rises 64 points then drops 110 points (44.15 pt loss)
Japan 1st exceeds military spending cap of 1% of GNP ($23 billion)
Bob Benoit bowls 1st 300-pt game in a televised title match
100s feared dead after a powerful earthquake strikes the Soviet Central Asian republic of Tajikistan
Challenge to "who is a Jew" law filed in Israeli Supreme Court
NBA New Jersey Nets begin a 32+ game road losing streak
Bagogwe Massacre of Tutsi in and around Ruhengeri, Northern Rwanda
World's largest oil spill, caused by embattled Iraqi forces in Kuwait
Indian Airlines B737 crashes at Aurangabad, killing 61
Worldwide Day for Peace in Bosnia-Herzegovina
The first version of the Java programming language released
Denver Nuggets tie the NBA single-season record of 23 consecutive losses set by the Vancouver Grizzlies in 1995-96; streak ends with a 99-81 win the next day against the LA Clippers
Dallas' Don Nelson becomes the 6th coach in NBA history to win 900 career games when the Mavericks defeat the Detroit Pistons, 99-91 at The Palace of Auburn Hills
Cave-in at coal mine leads to two methane gas explosions, kills 13 miners in Brookwood, Alabama
Five people set themselves on fire in Tiananmen Square, CCP uses the incident to escalate the persecution of Falun Gong
"American Taliban" John Walker Lindh returns to the United States in FBI custody
Reporter Daniel Pearl kidnapped in Karachi, Pakistan, and subsequently murdered.
Final communication between Earth and NASA space probe Pioneer 10 then 7.6 billion miles (12.23 billion kilometers) from Earth
Dendermonde nursery attack occurs in Dendermonde, Belgium
UK officially enters recession as GDP falls by 1.5% in the last quarter of 2008 following a 0.6% drop in the third quarter, with unemployment growing by 131,000 to 1.92 million (6.1%)
European Union agrees to embargo Iranian oil in protest against Iran's alleged nuclear weapons program
18 people are killed in a market shooting in Damboa, Nigeria
23 people are killed in a mosque suicide bombing in Tuz Khormato, Iraq
US armed forces overturns 1994 ban on women serving in combat
Category 5 blizzard delivers record 3ft of snow to the Mid-Atlantic and Northeast US, killing 55 and incurring estimated $500m - $3bn economic losses
Zika virus outbreak in Brazil prompts Colombia, Ecuador, El Salvador and Jamaica to recommend women delay pregnancies for up to 2 years
Most expensive house in the US worth $250 million goes on the market in Bel Air, Los Angeles
Thunderstorms and tornadoes in Georgia and Mississippi leave at least 18 dead in the US
Chinese researchers report they have cloned two monkeys, using the same technology as Dolly the Sheep
Twelve camels disqualified from the King Abdulaziz Camel beauty contest, Saudi Arabia after their owners used botox on their lips
New record for most expensive US property, $238 million for penthouse condominium overlooking Central Park, NY, bought by hedge fund billionaire Ken Griffin
75th anniversary of the liberation of the Auschwitz death camp marked by an international forum in Jerusalem, Israel
Voice of a 3,000 year old Egyptian priest recreated by scientists 3D printing his vocal tract published in "Scientific Reports"
Buffalo wide receiver Gabriel Davis scores an NFL playoff record 4 TDs in the Bills' epic 42-36 overtime loss to the Kansas City Chiefs in their divisional-round playoff at Arrowhead Stadium
American Richard Barnett, who was pictured with his feet on Nancy Pelosi's desk during the Jan 6 riots, convicted on eight counts in a Washington court
The Doomsday Clock remains set 90 seconds to midnight by the by Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists, siting threat of a new nuclear arms race, the Ukraine war and climate change
Former US representative John Ratcliffe is confirmed by the US Senate as Director of the CIA
Purdue Pharma and the Sackler family who own the company agree to pay $7.4 billion to settle new lawsuits over the harm caused by the opioid epidemic
Storm Éowyn hits Ireland and the UK with record-high winds of 114 mph (184 km/h), leaving hundreds of thousands without electricity in Northern Ireland and one person dead
Thailand becomes the 38th country and the first in Southeast Asia to legalize same-sex marriage, with more than 1,800 same-sex couples marrying
Beginning of the second Julian Period (1/1 OS)
Roman EmperorTheodosius Iproclaims his nine year old son Honorius co-emperor
In a coronation ceremony, Zhu Yuanzhang, aged 40, ascends to the throne of China as theHongwuEmperor, initiating Ming Dynasty rule over China that lasts for three centuries
Parliament of English KingRichard IIIopens, passes Titulus Regis, (Richard's right to the throne)
Queen Elizabeth Iof England opens Royal Exchange in London
New Model Army led byThomas Fairfaxcaptures Leeds for the Parliamentarians in the English Civil War
French philosopher and theologianBlaise Pascalpublishes the first of his "Lettres provinciales"
French astronomerCharles Messiercatalogs M56 (globular cluster in Lyra)
Elizabeth Blackwellbecomes 1st woman in the US to earn a medical degree graduating from New York's Geneva Medical College
John Wilkes Boothis baptized at St. Timothy's Protestant Episcopal Church
Daniel Hale Williamsforms the Provident Hospital in Chicago, the first non-segregated hospital in the US
Emilio Aguinaldois sworn in as President of the First Philippine Republic
Norwegian coastal town of Ålesund is devastated by fire, leaving 10,000 people homeless and one person dead. KaiserWilhelm IIfunds the rebuilding of the town in Jugendstil style.
US and Great Britain demand an end to atrocities in the Congo under KingLeopold II’s rule, prompting Belgium to annex it
The 'Young Turks' lead a coup d'etat against the Turkish Government, assassinating Minister of War Nazim Pasha
AfterStanley Baldwin's resignation,Ramsay MacDonaldforms Britain's first Labour Government, climaxing 24 years of struggle by the Labour Party
Eugene O'Neill's play "Great God Brown" premieres in NYC
A stage version of the Czech novel "The Adventure of the Good Soldier Schweik" produced and directed by Erwin Piscator with the help ofBertolt Brechtpremieres in Berlin
Karl Radek and sixteen others go on trial in Moscow as part of the Great Purge, many are sentenced to death
Duke Ellingtonplays at Carnegie Hall in New York City for the first time
World War II:Karl Dönitzlaunches Operation Hannibal to evacuate German soldiers and civilians from Prussia by sea
Pakistani cricket batsmanHanif Mohammadscores record 337 runs in 970 minutes in drawn 1st Test v West Indies at Bridgetown, longest innings in Test history
British intelligence officerKim Philbydefects to USSR
Arthur Miller's play "After the Fall" premieres in NYC
Boston Celtics' centerBill Russellmisses all 14 shots in 104-100 loss to Philadelphia 76ers, led by newly acquiredWilt Chamberlain
Stan Musialnamed General Manager of MLB team St. Louis Cardinals
Ard Schenkbecomes European all-round skating champion
Shah of IranMohammad Reza Pahlaviannounces that the 1954 operating agreement between a consortium of oil companies and Iran will not be renewed when it expires in 1979
RCA releases "Station to Station",David Bowie's 10th studio album, recorded in Los Angeles; it peaks in the U.S. charts at No.3, and No. 5 in the U.K.
NHL New York IslandersMike Bossybecomes 1st in NHL to score 50 goals in 50 games
Ivan Lendlscores a 6–4, 6-4, 6–2 win over AmericanJohn McEnroeto claim back-to-back ATP Masters Grand Prix tennis titles at Madison Square Garden, NYC
TV series "The A-Team" with George Peppard and Mr T premieres on NBC

Inaugural class of the Rock & Roll Hall of Fame inductees:Chuck Berry;James Brown;Ray Charles;Sam Cooke;Fats Domino; The Everly Brothers;Buddy Holly;Jerry Lee Lewis;Elvis Presley;Little Richard;Robert Johnson;Jimmie Rodgers; Jimmy Yancey; Alan Freed; John Hammond; and Sam Phillips
NY Newsday reports US Senator from OregonBob Packwoodsexually harassed 23 women

Michael Jordanscores in double figures for 800th consecutive game in a 100-98 OT win over New Jersey; adds 40 more double-figure scoring games to his NBA record before retiring for the second time (1998)
PopeJohn Paul IIcondemns US embargo against Cuba
"Numb3rs", starring Rob Morrow andDavid Krumholtz, debuts on CBS

Capitol Records releases "Black Cadillac", the eleventh studio album by singer-songwriterRosanne Cash; the album is dedicated to the memory of her recently deceased parents and step-mother, and co-produced by her husband John Leventhal and Bill Bottrell
Golden State guardKlay Thompsonscores the most NBA points in a quarter (37) and most 3-pointers in a quarter (9) in Warrior's 126-101 win over Sacramento Kings
Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saudis crowned King of Saudi Arabia, on the death of this half-brother King Abdullah
8 museum workers from Egyptian Museum, Cairo referred for prosecution for reattachingTutankhamun's beard with inappropriate glue
Formula One Group removesBernie Ecclestoneas CEO, making him chairman emeritus, as Liberty Media completes $8 billion takeover of the organization
American singer-songwriterNeil Diamondabruptly retires from touring after being diagnosed with Parkinson's Disease
LeBron Jamesbecomes the seventh and youngest player (33 years, 24 days) to reach the 30,000 NBA point milestone during a 114-102 loss to the San Antonio Spurs
US House SpeakerNancy Pelosidisinvites PresidentDonald Trumpfrom making a State of the Union address in Congress citing government shutdown
Venezuelan opposition leader Juan Guaido declares himself acting President amid widespread violent protests against President Nicolas Maduro's regime
Actress Annabella Sciorra testifies in court that movie mogulHarvey Weinsteinraped her 25 years ago at his trial in New York City
Japan on the brink of not being able to function as a society, because of its falling birth rate and high life expectancy, says Prime MinisterFumio Kishida, in speech to Japanese parliament
Kawhi Leonardrecords his second career triple-double in a win against the Los Angeles Lakers
US PresidentDonald Trumpissues pardons to 23 anti-abortion activists convicted of illegally blocking access to clinics
Second version of the Book of Common Prayer becomes mandatory in England
War of the First Coalition: French cavalry captures 14 Dutch ships and 850 guns near the port of Den Helder - rare instance of cavalry capturing a fleet
US PresidentRichard Nixonannounces an accord has been reached to end the Vietnam War
China locks down the city of Wuhan and its 9 million people in a belated but ultimately successful effort to control the city's COVID-19 outbreak
Vincent Ferrer, Spanish missionary and saint, born in Valencia, Aragon (d. 1419)
Lucas Franchoys the Elder, Belgian painter, born in Mechelen, Belgium (d. 1643)
Ulrika Eleonora, Queen of Sweden, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1741)
John Landen, English mathematician (elliptic integrals), born in Peakirk, England (d. 1790)
Joseph Hewes, American merchant (Declaration of Independence signatory), born in Princeton, New Jersey (d. 1779)
William Jessop, English canal engineer, born in Plymouth, United Kingdom (d. 1814)
Muzio Clementi, Italian-English pianist, composer, music publisher, pedagogue, and piano maker, born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1832)
Friedrich von Matthison, German poet (Adelaide), born in Hohendodeleben, Germany (d. 1831)
Christian A. Vulpius, German novelist and dramatist (Rinaldo Rinaldini), born in Weimar, Germany (d. 1827)
Stendhal [Marie Henri Beyle], French writer (Le Rouge et de Noir, a Chartreuse de Parme), born in Grenoble, France (d. 1842)
Carl Adolph Agardh, Swedish botanist & bishop of Karlstad, born in Båstad, Sweden (d. 1859)
Auguste de Montferrand, French architect, born in Trocadéro, Paris (d. 1858)
Veer Surendra Sai, Indian freedom fighter and tribal leader, born in Sambalpur district, India (d. 1884)
Franz Commer, German composer and musicologist, born in Cologne, Département de la Roer, First French Empire  (d. 1887)
Compars Herrmann, German stage magician, born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1887)
Alexander Serov, Russian composer and music critic, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1871)
Saigō Takamori, Japanese samurai & leader of Satsuma rebellion, born in Kajiyacho, Japan (d. 1877)
Calvin Edward Pratt, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Worcester County, Massachusetts (d. 1896)
Gaston, Marquis de Galliffet, French general and Minister of War (1899-1900), born in Paris, France (d. 1909)
John Randolph Chambliss Jr, American Brigadier-General (Confederate Army), born in Greensville County, Virginia (d. 1864)
Amanda Smith, American former slave, singer, and evangelist, born in Long Green, Maryland (d. 1915)
Ernst Abbe, German physicist and optical scientist (Carl Zeiss Optics Company), born in Eisenach, Germany (d. 1905)
Benoît-Constant Coquelin, French actor, born in Boulogne-sur-Mer, France (d. 1909)
Bolko von Hochberg, German diplomat and composer, born in Castle Fürstenstein, Silesia (d. 1926)
John Moses Browning, American weapons manufacturer, born in Ogden, Utah Territory (d. 1926)
Andrija Mohorovicic, Croatian meteorologist & seismologist (Moho discontinuity), born in Opatija, Croatia (d. 1936)
David Hilbert, German mathematician (Hilbert spaces, invariant theory, axiomatization of geometry), born in Königsberg, East Prussia (d. 1943)
Benjamin Delmonte, Dutch theater director and actor (Black Haired Whore), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1944)
Herbert Bedford, English author and composer, born in Holborn, London, England (d. 1945)
Juventino Rosas, Mexican composer and violinist, born in Santa Cruz de Juventino Rosas, Mexico (d. 1894)
Felice Boghen, Italian composer, born in Florence, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1945)
Herbert David Croly, American author (Promise of American Life), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1930)
William Morgan, American physical education instructor and inventor of "mintonette" (now known as volleyball), born in Lockport, New York (d. 1942)
Eugene Sullivan, American chemist and inventor (PYREX® Glass), born in Elgin, Illinois (d. 1962)[1]
Joze Plečnik, Slovenian architect, born in Ljubljana, Slovenia (d. 1957)
Paul Langevin, French physicist, born in Paris, France (d. 1946)
Bess Houdini, American-German stage assistant and wife of Harry Houdini, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1943)
Oton Zupancic, Slovenian poet (Zimzelen pod Snegom), born in Vinica, Črnomelj, Slovenia (d. 1949)
Robert Casa [Casadesus], French stage and film actor, singer, and composer, born in France (d. 1940)
Rutland Boughton, British composer (The Immortal Hour), born in Aylesbury, Buckinghamshire, England (d. 1960)
Boleslaw Wallek-Walewski, Polish composer and conductor, born in Lviv, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1944)
Gilbert Ledward, English sculptor, born in Chelsea, London (d. 1960)
Paul Peter Ewald, German Crystallographer and physicist who devised the theory of X-ray interference by crystals and pioneered X-ray diffraction methods, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1985)
Franklin Pangborn, American actor (My Best Gal, Hats Off, Easy Living), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1958)
Jüri Lossmann, Estonian athlete (Olympic silver marathon 1920), born in Kabala, Estonia (d. 1984)
Frank Carlson, American politician (Gov/Rep/Sen-R-Ks), born in Cloud County, Kansas (d. 1987)
Remy Angenot, Flemish actor (Met den helm geboren, Patriot, Idiot), born in Antwerpen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1964)
Alf Hall, South African cricket fast bowler (7 Tests, 40 wickets), born in Bolton, Lancashire, England (d. 1964)
Charlotte, Grand Duchess of Luxembourg (1919-64), born in Berg Castle, Luxembourg (d. 1985)
Margarete Schütte-Lihotsky, Austria's first female architect who designed the first fitted kitchen, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2000)[1]
Subhas Chandra Bose, Indian politician and nationalist, born in Cuttack, British India (d. 1945)
William Stephenson, Canadian soldier & spymaster, born in Winnipeg, Canada (d. 1989)
Freda Utley, British scholar and author, born in London (d. 1978)
Georg Kulenkampff, German concert violinist, born in Bremen, German Empire (d. 1948)
Randolph Scott, American actor (Ride the High Country, Last of the Mohicans, Western Union), born in Orange County, Virginia (d. 1987)
Glen Kidston, British aviator & racing driver, born in London (d. 1931)
Joseph Nathan Kane, American non-fiction writer and journalist (Famous Firsts), born in New York City (d. 2002)
Mary Philips, American actress (Farewell to Arms, Incendiary Blonde), born in New London, Connecticut (d. 1975)
Ralph Graves, American actor (Dirigible, Extra Girl, Flight, Dream Street), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1977)
William Ifor Jones, Welsh conductor and organist, born in Merthyr Tydfil, Wales (d. 1988)
Benno Stokvis, Dutch lawyer and politician, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Benny Waters, American jazz saxophonist & clarinetist, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1998)
Jorge Eliécer Gaitán, Colombian politician, born in Cucunubá, Colombia (d. 1948)
Theodor Schaefer, Czech composer and pedagogue, born in Telč, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1969)
David Newell, American actor (Runaway Bride, White Heat, Dangerous Curves), born in Carthage, Missouri (d. 1980)
Jerrold R. Zacharias, American physicist who designed the first atomic clock, born in Jacksonville, Florida (d. 1986)
Konstanty Ildefons Gałczyński, Polish poet (Zielona Ges), born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1953)
Bob Steele [Robert Adrian Bradbury], American actor (Monogram, Supreme, Tiffany), born in Portland, Oregon (d. 1988)
Dan Duryea, American actor (Pride of the Yankees), born in White Plains, New York (d. 1968)
Hideki Yukawa, Japanese physicist (Nobel 1949), born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1981)
Tatiana Proskouriakoff, Russian-American Mayan archaeologist who helped decipher Maya hieroglyphs, born in Tomsk, Russia (d. 1985)
Dan J. Smith, English harmonica player, and gospel singer-songwriter, born in London (d. 1994)
Napoleon L. Bonaparte, French pretender to the throne, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1997)
Herma Bauma, Austrian javelin thrower (Olympic gold 1948), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2003)
Potter Stewart, United States Supreme Court justice (1958-81), born in Jackson, Michigan (d. 1985)
William Arthur Lewis, Saint Lucian economist (1979 Nobel Prize in Economics for economic development research), born in Castries, Saint Lucia (d. 1991)[1]
Airey Neave, British soldier and politician, born in Knightsbridge, London (d. 1979)
David Douglas Duncan, American photo-journalist best known for his war photography, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2018)
Dusan Kostic, Serbian writer, born in Peja, Serbia (d. 1997)
Bob Paisley, English football player and manager, born in Hetton-le-Hole, United Kingdom (d. 1996)
Ernie Kovacs, American comedian (The Ernie Kovacs Show), born in Trenton, New Jersey (d. 1962)
Frances Bay, Canadian-American character actress (Twin Peaks - "Mrs. Tremond"), born in Manville, Alberta (d. 2011)
Hans Hass, Austrian zoologist and underwater scientist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2013)
Nina Dumbadze, Soviet discus thrower (Olympic bronze 1952), born in Odessa, Ukraine (d. 1983)
Gottfried Böhm, German architect (Pritzker Prize, 1986), born in Offenbach am Main, Germany (d. 2021)[1]
Florence Halop, American actress (Night Court, 1985-86 -"Florence"; St Elsewhere, 1984-85), born in Jamaica, Queens, New York (d. 1986)
Horace Ashenfelter, American athlete (Olympic gold 3000m steeplechase 1952), born in Phoenixville, Pennsylvania (d. 2018)
Walter M. Miller Jr., American sci-fi author (Hugo; View from Stars), born in New Smyrna Beach, Florida (d. 1996)
Frank Lautenberg, American politician (Sen-D-NJ, 1982-2001, 2003-2013), born in Paterson, New Jersey (d. 2013)
Marty Paich, American jazz pianist, conductor, producer, and arranger (Frank Sinatra, Barbra Streisand, Ella Fitzgerald, Aretha Franklin, Michael Jackson), born in Oakland, California (d. 1995)
Lars-Eric Lindblad, Swedish-American entrepreneur and explorer, born in Solna Municipality, Sweden (d. 1994)
Chico Carrasquel, Venezuelan baseball player, born in Caracas, Venezuela (d. 2005)
Elayne Jones, American classical timpanist, and civil rights activist, born in Harlem, New York City (d. 2022)[1]
Eugenio Monti, Italian bobsledder (2 x Olympic gold 2-man, 4-man 1968; World C'ships 9 x gold), born in Toblach, Italy (d. 2003)
Jeanne Moreau, French actress (Going Places, Jules & Jim), born in Paris, France (d. 2017)
Kees Broekman, Dutch speed skater (Olympic silver 1952), born in De Lier, Netherlands (d. 1992)
Ken Errair, American pop-jazz singer (Four Freshmen, 1953-55 - "Graduation Day"), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1968)
Filaret Denysenko [Mykhailo Denysenko], Ukrainian religious leader and Patriarch Filaret of the Ukrainian Orthodox Church - Kyiv Patriarchate, born in Blahodatne, Donetsk, Ukrainian SSR
Ian Thomson, English cricket fast bowler (5 Tests, 9 wickets; Sussex), born in Walsall, England (d. 2021)
Myron Cope, American sports broadcaster ("the voice of the Pittsburgh Steelers" 1970-2005), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2008)
Derek Walcott, Caribbean poet (Omeros), Nobel Prize for Literature 1992, born in Castries, St Lucia (d. 2017)
